preFix.title: Throwing a matched nested bowl set
preFix.subtitle: Five stoneware bowls in graduated sizes that stack inside each other
preFix.excerpt: A set of five stoneware bowls ranging from 120 mm to 240 mm in diameter, all thrown with the same wall angle so they nest cleanly. The discipline is replicating consistent proportions across five separate throws. A profile gauge and written dimension notes are used to keep each bowl within tolerance. All five are glazed identically and fired to cone 6.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Never open a hot kiln below approximately 80 degrees C.

A nested bowl set is above all a precision project: five bowls thrown in smooth stoneware, in diameters of 120 mm, 150 mm, 180 mm, 210 mm, and 240 mm, with a consistent wall angle on every piece so they stack inside each other without gaps. The challenge is not throwing any single bowl well, but throwing five bowls that are related to each other. A profile gauge, calipers, and a written dimension card used alongside the wheel are the tools that make this achievable. All five bowls are glazed identically and fired to
cone
6 (approximately 1220 degrees C).
Preparation

Weigh out five balls of stoneware: 250 g, 350 g, 500 g, 650 g, and 800 g. These weights produce the five target diameters with walls of approximately 6 mm. Throw the largest bowl first as your reference piece. Make a profile gauge immediately after throwing it: press a piece of stiff card against the inside curve of the wet bowl and trace the profile with a pencil. This card gauge gives you a physical template to compare each subsequent bowl against as you work. Write down the rim diameter, base diameter, and wall height for each bowl as you throw it.

materials.name: Mid-fire glaze, pre-mixed
materials.quantity: enough to dip all five bowls

content.text: Throwing the body

Throw the largest bowl (800 g, 240 mm diameter) first. Centre the clay and open a wide floor: the base of the bowl should be approximately 80 mm across and the floor compressed firmly before pulling the walls.

Pull the walls outward and upward at a consistent angle of approximately 25 degrees from vertical. The bowl wall should curve continuously from base to rim without a sharp transition. Use the wooden rib on the outside of the wall to refine the curve and compress the clay.

Measure the rim diameter with calipers. The wet clay will shrink approximately 12 percent during drying and firing, so throw the rim to 272 mm to achieve a finished 240 mm bowl. Record the actual thrown diameter, not the target finished diameter, on your dimension card.

Immediately after throwing the first bowl, hold your card gauge against the inside profile and trace the curve. This is your reference for all subsequent bowls. Wire the bowl off the bat and set it aside.

Throw the remaining four bowls in descending order, checking each profile against the card gauge after throwing. The curve of the inner wall, not the absolute size, is what determines whether the bowls nest: a shallower curve in one bowl will cause a gap when the next bowl sits inside it.

For each subsequent bowl, calculate the thrown diameter by dividing the target finished diameter by 0.88 (to account for 12 percent shrinkage). Record the actual thrown measurement for each bowl on your dimension card as you go.

Smooth the rim of each bowl with a chamois. Compress the base of each bowl at the same stage of throwing for consistency. Wire each bowl off its bat and allow all five to dry together in the same area of the studio to ensure equal drying rates.
Trimming
Trim all five bowls at the same session, when all have reached the
leather-hard

stage. Use a foot ring chuck to hold each bowl securely while inverted on the wheel. A chuck is a tall collar of clay centred on the wheel head into which the bowl sits rim-down; it prevents the bowl from sliding off-centre during trimming.

Trim a matching foot ring profile on each bowl. The foot ring width and height should be proportionally consistent across all five bowls, not identical in size. For the largest bowl, a foot ring 8 mm wide and 6 mm tall is a good guide. Scale the size down for the smaller bowls.
foot ring

profile on each bowl: the foot ring width and height should be proportionally consistent across all five bowls, not identical in absolute measurement. For the largest bowl, a foot ring 8 mm wide and 6 mm tall is appropriate; scale proportionally downward for the smaller bowls.

After trimming each bowl, test the nest: place the trimmed bowl right-side up on the banding wheel and stack the others inside it. The foot ring of the bowl above should rest on the inner wall of the bowl below without the walls touching. If two bowls are contacting wall-to-wall, the inner profile is too steep on one of them: adjust by widening the base slightly on a subsequent bowl.
Drying and bisque firing

Cover all five bowls with a large sheet of loose plastic for 24 to 2 days to slow drying. The smallest bowl dries faster than the largest; covering them together slows the faster bowls to the rate of the slowest. Remove the plastic and allow all pieces to reach
bone-dry
. This is
greenware
: handle each bowl by the base, not the rim. Load all five in the bisque kiln and fire to cone 06 (approximately 1000 degrees C) to produce
bisque
. The bowls can be stacked inside each other in the bisque firing to save kiln space, because bisque pieces do not stick to each other.
Glazing
Glaze all five bowls with the same pre-mixed mid-fire glaze using the same technique to ensure a consistent colour result. The glaze must be thoroughly stirred between each bowl.

Dip each bowl rim-down into the glaze bucket to coat the interior and exterior in one movement, holding for three seconds, then lifting cleanly. The foot ring and the bottom 5 mm of the exterior remain unglazed.

Wipe the foot ring of each bowl with a damp sponge after dipping to remove any accidental glaze coverage. In the glaze firing, the bowls cannot be stacked inside each other: each bowl sits on its own foot ring on the shelf or on kiln stilts.
Glaze firing

Fire all five bowls to cone 6 (approximately 1220 degrees C) in the same load to guarantee that all five pieces receive identical heat-work and the glaze colour is consistent across the set. Cool naturally. Once cool, test the nest again: some warping during firing is normal, and if one bowl no longer nests cleanly, check whether the rim or the foot ring of the bowl above is catching and address with a fine diamond pad on the specific contact point.
Care

The bowls are food-safe and dishwasher-safe at cone 6. Stack them nested for storage: the foot ring of each bowl sits on the interior wall of the bowl below without touching the glaze surface to glaze surface, which would scratch both. Do not store them stacked on top of each other rim to rim, as the rims are the thinnest and most fragile part of the set.
Variations
Graduated glaze: apply the same glaze in progressively thicker coats from the smallest to the largest bowl, so the colour deepens across the set.
Three-bowl set: for a first attempt, reduce to three bowls at 120 mm, 180 mm, and 240 mm. Fewer pieces means fewer variables to control while the discipline of matching profiles is still learned.
Serving and prep set: throw the three largest bowls as deep mixing bowls and the two smallest as prep bowls with a pouring lip added at leather-hard for versatility.

rows.fix: Compare the card profile gauge against the bowl wall after throwing; if the curve does not match, adjust on the wheel before the clay stiffens
rows.cause: The inner wall profile is inconsistent: one bowl has a steeper or shallower curve than the others
rows.problem: Bowls do not nest: one bowl sits too high in the next

rows.fix: Keep all five bowls covered with plastic for the first 2 days and in the same slow-drying environment
rows.cause: Smaller pieces dry much faster than larger ones, and the differential drying creates stress
rows.problem: Smallest bowls crack at the rim during drying

rows.fix: Fire all five bowls in the same kiln load; stir glaze between each dip; hold each piece in the glaze for the same length of time
rows.cause: Glaze was applied at different thicknesses, or the bowls were fired in different kiln loads at different cone levels
rows.problem: Finished glaze colour is noticeably different between bowls in the set

rows.fix: Check wall thickness at multiple points before drying; fire with the rims level, not tilted; load away from elements
rows.cause: Uneven wall thickness, or the bowls were placed too close to a kiln element
rows.problem: Bowls warp out of round during firing
